data.sentence: Okay, I think we've got everyone. Tom, you there? Brian's on, Ananya's on — Sofia, can you confirm?
Yeah I'm here, sorry, I was just pulling up the dashboards.
Good. Okay, let's not waste time. Tom, do you want to kick us off or should I?

I'll start. So, uh, at approximately 9:14 AM Pacific we started seeing alerts — or rather, the absence of alerts — from the Detect event processing pipeline. The ingestion layer just... stopped. No events coming through, no throughput, and the monitoring didn't catch it immediately which is its own problem we're going to talk about.
When you say the monitoring didn't catch it — are we talking about our internal alerting or the customer-facing status stuff?
Both, honestly. Internal alerting had a lag, and the status page wasn't updated until... Ananya, when did we actually post to the status page?

It was around 10:40, so that's — yeah, that's about an hour and a half after the initial failure. And I want to be upfront, that's on me partly because I was the one who was supposed to pull the trigger on that and I was heads-down trying to figure out the root cause first.
Ananya, I appreciate you saying that but that's a process failure, not a you failure. We shouldn't have a single person making the call on when to post. That's a systemic issue.

I mean — I agree with that, but we can talk process later. Right now I've got customers blowing up my team's queue and I need to understand what actually happened technically so I can give my support engineers something to say.
Right, yeah. Sofia, you want to walk through the technical failure? You were in the weeds on this from the start.

Sure. So the event ingestion pipeline — basically it's a single primary node that handles all incoming threat event data before it fans out to the processing workers. That node hit a memory ceiling, started throwing OOM errors, and because we don't have a circuit breaker or any redundancy on that node, it just cascaded. Everything downstream starved out.
Wait, so there's literally one node doing all of the ingestion? That's... okay I'm not an engineer but that sounds like a really bad design.
It's not ideal, no. It's — look, it's a legacy architecture decision and it's been on the roadmap to address. We just hadn't prioritized it.
It was flagged in the Q4 tech debt review, Tom. I flagged it.
I know you did. And I'm not — look, this is not the time for that conversation. Yes, it was flagged, yes, it should have been prioritized, and now we're dealing with the consequences. I own that.
Okay. So what's the current status? Are customers seeing data right now or are we still down?

We're still down as of this call. Sofia and I have been working on a hotfix — we're spinning up a secondary ingestion node and routing traffic through a temporary load balancer config, but it's not clean. It's a stopgap.
How long until that stopgap is live?
Best case? Another hour, maybe ninety minutes. But I don't want to commit to that publicly yet.
Brian, what are you hearing from customers? Like, who's screaming the loudest right now?

I mean, everyone in the enterprise tier is screaming. We've got — let me just look at my notes here — Harmon Financial has escalated twice, they're threatening to call their account manager directly. TerraLogix is asking whether this is a breach, which is a great question to get at 10 in the morning. And we've got at least a dozen mid-market customers who are just, you know, sitting there with a broken security product and no visibility into their threat environment.
TerraLogix thinks it's a breach? We need to get ahead of that immediately.

I know, I've got one of my senior support reps on the phone with them right now. But Tom, I need an actual statement I can give customers about what happened. Something honest that doesn't make us look like we don't know what we're doing.
The problem is we're still root-causing. I don't want to put out a statement that's wrong.
Tom, we know enough. It's a pipeline failure. It's not a breach. That's the most important thing to communicate right now. Customers would rather have an incomplete honest update than silence.
I agree with Brian on this one. We've been in communications blackout for too long already.
Fine. Fine. I'll draft something in the next — can someone give me fifteen minutes and I'll have a customer statement?
Please. And make sure it explicitly says this is not a security breach, that's the first question everyone's asking.
Yeah, I will. Sofia, while I'm doing that — can you document what you know so far about the failure sequence? We're going to need a timeline for the post-mortem.

Already started. I've got a doc in Confluence, I'll drop the link in Slack. One thing I want to flag though — when we bring the stopgap online, customers are going to start seeing alerts again but there may be a gap in their event history. Like, events that occurred during the outage window may not be recoverable. We need to be upfront about that.
Oh, that's — yeah, that's going to be a very bad conversation to have with customers in regulated industries. Are we sure about that? Is there any way to replay the events?
We have some buffering at the source layer but it's limited. I'd say we can recover maybe sixty to seventy percent of what was lost. The rest is... gone.
Okay. That's — we need to think carefully about how we communicate that. That's a significant data loss event for a security monitoring product.
Agreed. Let's not bury it. Put it in the post-incident report. Customers deserve to know.

Can we talk about the immediate next steps? I just want to make sure we have a clear owner on each thing before we get off this call, because the last two hours have been a lot of people doing a lot of things without coordination.

Yes. Okay. Sofia and Ananya, you own the stopgap fix and the technical timeline doc. Brian, you own customer communications and escalation triage. I'll draft the public statement and then I'm going to get on a call with the CTO because she's going to need to know about this if she doesn't already.
She knows. I got a Slack from her about forty minutes ago.
Great. That's... great. Okay. Let's reconvene in ninety minutes or sooner if the fix goes live. Everybody check in on Slack. And — I know this has been a rough morning. Let's just get it fixed.
Agreed. Let's go.
